section.title: Rahab and the Spies
section.sectionRationale: Before Israel crosses the Jordan, Joshua sends two spies into Jericho, and the story turns unexpectedly on a Canaanite prostitute. Rahab hides the spies, confesses that the LORD is God of heaven and earth, and pleads for her family to be spared. A covenant is struck, sealed by a scarlet cord, and the spies return with a report not of Israel's strength but of God's terror already fallen on the land. It is one movement, the surprising conversion and rescue of an outsider on the eve of conquest.
section.thesis: On the threshold of a war of judgment, God shows that His purpose is not the destruction of every Canaanite but the salvation of all who take refuge in Him, as a pagan prostitute, hearing what God has done, believes, acts on her faith, and is drawn by grace into the covenant people.
section.frameNote: Four beats: the spies enter and Rahab hides them, verses 1 to 7; Rahab's confession of the LORD, verses 8 to 11; the covenant and the scarlet cord, verses 12 to 21; and the spies' report that the land is already melting in fear, verses 22 to 24.

units.label: The spies enter and Rahab hides them
units.note: she hid them among the flax on the roof

verses.text: Then Joshua son of Nun secretly sent two spies from Shittim, saying, “Go, inspect the land, especially Jericho.” So they went and entered the house of a prostitute named Rahab and stayed there.
And it was reported to the king of Jericho: “Behold, some men of Israel have come here tonight to spy out the land.”
So the king of Jericho sent to Rahab and said, “Bring out the men who came to you and entered your house, for they have come to spy out the whole land.”
But the woman had taken the two men and hidden them. So she said, “Yes, the men did come to me, but I did not know where they had come from.
At dusk, when the gate was about to close, the men went out, and I do not know which way they went. Pursue them quickly, and you may catch them!”
(But Rahab had taken them up to the roof and hidden them among the stalks of flax that she had laid out there.)
So the king’s men set out in pursuit of the spies along the road to the fords of the Jordan, and as soon as they had gone out, the gate was shut.

units.label: Rahab's confession of the LORD
units.note: the LORD your God is God in heaven above and on earth below

verses.text: Before the spies lay down for the night, Rahab went up on the roof
and said to them, “I know that the LORD has given you this land and that the fear of you has fallen on us, so that all who dwell in the land are melting in fear of you.

For we have heard how the LORD dried up the waters of the Red Sea before you when you came out of Egypt, and what you did to Sihon and Og, the two kings of the Amorites across the Jordan, whom you {{cherem|devoted to destruction}}.
When we heard this, our hearts melted and everyone’s courage failed because of you, for the LORD your God is God in the heavens above and on the earth below.

units.label: The covenant and the scarlet cord
units.note: swear to me by the LORD; the scarlet cord in the window

verses.text: Now therefore, please swear to me by the LORD that you will indeed show {{chesed|kindness}} to my family, because I showed kindness to you. Give me a sure sign
that you will spare the lives of my father and mother, my brothers and sisters, and all who belong to them, and that you will deliver us from death.”
“Our lives for your lives!” the men agreed. “If you do not report our mission, we will show you kindness and {{emeth|faithfulness}} when the LORD gives us the land.”
Then Rahab let them down by a rope through the window, since the house where she lived was built into the wall of the city.
“Go to the hill country,” she said, “so that your pursuers will not find you. Hide yourselves there for three days until they have returned; then go on your way.”
The men said to her, “We will not be bound by this oath you made us swear

unless, when we enter the land, you have tied this scarlet cord in the window through which you let us down, and unless you have brought your father and mother and brothers and all your family into your house.

If anyone goes out the door of your house into the street, his blood will be on his own head, and we will be innocent. But if a hand is laid on anyone with you in the house, his blood will be on our heads.
And if you report our mission, we will be released from the oath you made us swear.”
“Let it be as you say,” she replied, and she sent them away. And when they had gone, she tied the scarlet cord in the window.

units.label: The spies report the land melting in fear
units.note: the LORD has surely delivered the entire land into our hands

verses.text: So the spies went out into the hill country and stayed there three days, until their pursuers had returned without finding them, having searched all along the road.
Then the two men started back, came down from the hill country, and crossed the river. So they came to Joshua son of Nun and reported all that had happened to them.
“The LORD has surely delivered the entire land into our hands,” they said to Joshua. “Indeed, all who dwell in the land are melting in fear of us.”

cherem.gloss: That which is devoted to destruction, set apart irrevocably to the LORD, whether by destroying it or offering it. Rahab has heard how Israel devoted Sihon and Og to destruction. The word names the solemn, God-ordained judgment about to fall on Canaan; the wonder of this chapter is that a woman who names this very judgment is herself spared, drawn out from under the ban by faith.

chesed.gloss: Steadfast love, covenant loyalty, kindness that keeps faith. Rahab asks the spies to show her chesed because she has shown chesed to them, and they swear it. This is the great covenant word used again and again of the LORD's own loyal love; here it binds an Israelite oath to a Canaanite believer, a picture of covenant mercy reaching beyond Israel.

emeth.gloss: Truth, faithfulness, reliability. Paired with chesed, kindness and faithfulness, verse 14, it forms the well-known covenant couplet describing dependable, promise-keeping love. The spies pledge not mere goodwill but faithfulness, a sworn commitment they will keep, mirroring the steadfast love and truth of the God in whose name they swear.

basic.context: Joshua sends two spies into Jericho, and they end up in the house of Rahab, a Canaanite prostitute. When the king's men come looking, Rahab hides the spies and sends the search off in the wrong direction. Then she tells the spies why: she has heard how the LORD rescued Israel from Egypt and defeated the kings east of the Jordan, and she now believes that the LORD is God of heaven and earth. She asks that when Israel takes the city, her family will be spared. The spies swear an oath, sealed by a scarlet cord she must tie in her window. They return to Joshua reporting that the land is already terrified, because God has gone ahead of them. The question at the heart of it is: who can be saved when God's judgment comes?
basic.hermeneutics: This is historical narrative, and it sits inside the hard reality of the conquest, the herem, God's judgment on Canaan. The chapter is not primarily about spy craft or even about Rahab's lie, which the text neither commends as a rule nor dwells on, but about faith. Rahab is held up in the New Testament as an example of saving faith that acts, James 2:25, and as a woman justified by faith, Hebrews 11:31. We read her story as a bright thread of mercy running through a chapter about judgment.
basic.originalLanguages: Rahab names the cherem, the devotion to destruction, that fell on Sihon and Og and is coming to Canaan. Yet she asks for chesed, covenant kindness, and the spies swear chesed and emeth, kindness and faithfulness, the great couplet of God's own loyal love. The vocabulary of judgment and the vocabulary of covenant mercy meet in one Canaanite woman.
basic.typology: The scarlet cord in Rahab's window, marking the house where her family will be safe when destruction sweeps the city, recalls the blood on the doorposts at Passover and points to the blood of Christ, under which sinners are sheltered from judgment. Rahab herself, an outsider brought into Israel, foreshadows the nations gathered into God's people through faith.
basic.christ: Rahab, a Gentile and a prostitute, is saved by faith and welcomed into Israel, and she appears in the very genealogy of Jesus, Matthew 1:5. She is a living sign that Christ came to save sinners and outsiders, and that no one is beyond His mercy. The scarlet cord that shelters her household points to the blood of Christ, the sure sign under which all who believe are spared.
basic.god: God is the sovereign Judge whose acts in Egypt and beyond the Jordan have already gone ahead of Israel, melting the courage of Canaan. He is God in heaven above and on earth below, as even a pagan woman confesses. And He is the God of mercy, who does not delight in the death of the wicked but saves the one who turns to Him, even a Canaanite prostitute on the eve of judgment.
basic.crossReferences: Hebrews 11 lists Rahab among the heroes of faith. James 2 points to her as faith proved by works. Matthew 1 places her in the family tree of Jesus. And Exodus 12 tells of the blood on the doorposts, the pattern the scarlet cord recalls.
basic.oneStory: As Israel stands ready to enter the land under God's judgment, God plucks one believing outsider out of a doomed city and brings her into His people. Rahab's rescue by faith, sealed with a scarlet cord, points ahead to the salvation of the nations and to the blood of Christ under which every believing sinner is sheltered.

context.literary: Chapter 2 pauses the forward march to Canaan for a self-contained story that sets the tone for the conquest. It is framed by the spies' departure and return, and its center of gravity is not military intelligence but Rahab's confession, verses 9 to 11, which repeats the language of God's mighty acts. The scarlet cord and the repeated melting in fear tie the chapter together, and the spies' closing report deliberately contrasts with the faithless report of the ten spies a generation earlier, Numbers 13.
context.historical: Jericho was a fortified city guarding the approach into Canaan from the Jordan valley. A house built into the city wall, and a woman like Rahab whose trade would bring travelers and news, make her a plausible source and refuge for spies. The memory of the Red Sea and the defeats of Sihon and Og, some forty years and just recently, shows how reports of the LORD's acts had spread through Canaan and terrified its peoples, exactly as Exodus 15:15-16 had foretold. Flax drying on a flat roof and city gates shut at dusk fit the setting precisely.
context.canonical: Rahab becomes one of Scripture's great examples of saving faith, named in Hebrews 11 and James 2, and one of the few women named in the genealogy of Jesus, Matthew 1. Her story stands with other accounts of outsiders brought into Israel by faith, and it anticipates the New Testament gospel going to the nations. Set against the coming judgment on Canaan, her rescue shows that the conquest was never about ethnicity but about faith and covenant, and that mercy was always available to those who turned to the LORD.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To show that on the eve of the conquest God saves a believing outsider out of a condemned city, to hold up Rahab's faith that acts as a pattern, and to assure Israel through the spies' report that the LORD has already given the land, so the coming victory rests on God, not on Israel's might.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes Rahab's actions, including her deception of the king's men, without turning them into a general rule for believers; the text's commendation, echoed in Hebrews and James, is of her faith and her welcoming of the spies, not a lesson in when to lie. What is prescriptive is that saving faith hears what God has done, believes He is the true God, and acts, casting itself on His mercy.

debates.question: Does the chapter approve of Rahab's lie to the king of Jericho?

views.label: Her faith is commended, not her method
views.case: Scripture praises Rahab for her faith and for receiving the spies, Hebrews 11:31 and James 2:25, never singling out the lie for approval; the narrative reports it as part of the account without holding it up as a model, so we honor her faith while not treating deception as a rule to follow.

views.label: The deception is a lesser act within a life-and-death loyalty
views.case: Some argue that Rahab, having sided with God's people against a city under judgment, acted to protect life in a wartime setting, so that her concealment is viewed sympathetically as the outworking of a new and imperfect faith turning from her old allegiances toward the LORD.

debates.bearing: Both agree the point of the passage is Rahab's God-given faith that acts; the text leaves her deception without commendation and directs our attention to the mercy of God that saves her.

debates.question: How can the conquest of Canaan, its herem judgment, be reconciled with God's goodness?

views.label: Just judgment on entrenched wickedness, with mercy always open
views.case: The conquest is presented in Scripture as God's righteous judgment on centuries of Canaanite evil, Genesis 15:16 and Deuteronomy 9:4-5, a judgment He delayed for generations; Rahab shows that the door of mercy stood open to any who would turn to the LORD, so the war was never about ethnicity but about faith and unrepentant sin.

views.label: A unique, bounded act in redemptive history
views.case: The herem was a limited, God-commanded event tied to a particular land and moment, not a pattern for later warfare; it dramatizes the seriousness of sin and the holiness of God, and it points forward to the final judgment, while the church's warfare is spiritual, not by the sword, 2 Corinthians 10:4.

debates.bearing: Held honestly together, these guard both truths: God's judgment on sin is real and just, and His mercy genuinely reaches even a Canaanite prostitute who believes, which is precisely why this chapter stands where it does.

study.originalLanguages: The chapter sets two vocabularies side by side and lets them meet in one woman. Rahab has heard how Israel devoted Sihon and Og to cherem, the devotion to destruction, the solemn, irrevocable judgment now bearing down on Canaan; she names the very doom hanging over her own city. Yet in the same breath she appeals to chesed, steadfast covenant love, asking the spies to show kindness to her family because she showed kindness to them, and they swear to show her chesed and emeth, kindness and faithfulness, the great couplet Scripture uses again and again for the LORD's own loyal, promise-keeping love, Exodus 34:6. The word of judgment and the word of covenant mercy meet in Rahab, and mercy wins: the one who confesses that the LORD is God in heaven above and on earth below is drawn out from under the ban and bound instead by an oath of steadfast love. Her confession itself echoes the language of Deuteronomy 4:39, so that a Canaanite outsider speaks better theology than many in Israel.

typology.type: The scarlet cord marking the house to be spared in the city's destruction
typology.fulfillment: The blood of the Passover lamb on the doorposts, and ultimately the blood of Christ, under which believers are sheltered from judgment, Exodus 12:13 and 1 Peter 1:18-19.

typology.type: Rahab the Canaanite outsider brought into Israel by faith
typology.fulfillment: The nations gathered into the people of God through faith in Christ, and Rahab herself in the line of the Messiah, Matthew 1:5 and Ephesians 2:12-13.

typology.type: The LORD's terror going ahead to melt the land before Israel arrives
typology.fulfillment: God going before His people to accomplish what they cannot, the victory belonging to Him, Romans 8:31 and 1 Corinthians 15:57.

study.christ: Rahab is a Gentile, a prostitute, a citizen of a city under God's judgment, and she is saved, wholly by grace, through a faith that hears what God has done and acts on it. That alone preaches the gospel: Christ came not for the righteous but for sinners, and no one is too far gone for His mercy. But the pointer runs deeper. The scarlet cord in her window marks out the one house where destruction will pass over, just as the blood of the lamb marked the doorposts in Egypt, and both point to the blood of Christ, the sure sign under which every believing sinner is spared the judgment to come. And Rahab does not merely survive; she is grafted into Israel, marries into Judah's line, and stands in the genealogy of Jesus Himself, Matthew 1:5, a standing witness that Christ delights to save and honor outsiders who trust Him.
study.god: This chapter reveals God as both Judge and Savior. He is the sovereign LORD whose acts at the Red Sea and against Sihon and Og have already run ahead of His people, so that the fear of Him has fallen on Canaan and its courage has melted; the victory is decided before a single Israelite crosses the river. He is God in heaven above and on earth below, as a pagan woman is given grace to confess. And He is merciful beyond Israel's borders: He does not delight in the death of the wicked but plucks a believing prostitute and her household out of a doomed city. The same God who judges Canaan's entrenched evil opens a door of rescue to anyone who will turn to Him, showing that His judgments are righteous and His mercy is wide.

crossReferences.note: By faith Rahab the prostitute did not perish with the disobedient, because she welcomed the spies in peace.
Rahab is justified by works, her faith shown alive by receiving and protecting the messengers.
Rahab appears in the genealogy of Jesus, mother of Boaz, an outsider brought into the Messianic line.
The blood on the doorposts causes judgment to pass over, the pattern the scarlet cord recalls.
God waited until the iniquity of the Amorites was complete before bringing judgment through Israel, showing His long patience.

study.oneStory: As Israel stands on the edge of the promised land and the judgment of God is about to fall on Canaan, God reaches into a doomed city and saves one believing outsider and her whole household. Rahab hears what the LORD has done, confesses Him as God of heaven and earth, and ties a scarlet cord in her window. Her rescue by faith is a preview of the gospel: the nations brought into God's people, sinners sheltered under the blood, and even a Canaanite prostitute honored in the family line of Jesus the Messiah.

quiz.q: What did Rahab confess about the LORD to the spies?

options.text: That the LORD had given Israel the land and is God in heaven above and on earth below.
options.note: Verses 9 to 11 record her remarkable confession of faith in the true God.

options.text: That the God of Israel was one powerful god among the gods of the nations.
options.note: She goes further than this, confessing the LORD as God of heaven and earth, not merely a strong tribal deity.

options.text: That she hoped Israel's God might protect her city.
options.note: She knew the city was doomed; her plea was to be saved out of it, not that God would spare Jericho.

options.text: That she did not really believe but only wanted a bargain.
options.note: Scripture treats her words as genuine saving faith, Hebrews 11:31.

quiz.q: Why had the people of Jericho lost their courage?

options.text: They had heard how the LORD dried up the Red Sea and defeated Sihon and Og, and the fear of Him had fallen on them.
options.note: Verses 9 to 11 show God's past acts had already melted the land in fear.

options.text: They had seen how large and well-armed Israel's army was.
options.note: Their fear was of what the LORD had done, not of Israel's military size; the text credits God's acts.

options.text: Their king had died and left them leaderless.
options.note: The king is still active in the chapter; their dread came from reports of the LORD's power.

options.text: A famine had weakened the city.
options.note: No famine is mentioned; their courage failed because of what they heard the LORD had done.

quiz.q: What was the sign that would spare Rahab's household?

options.text: A scarlet cord tied in her window, with her family gathered inside the house.
options.note: Verses 18 to 21 make the scarlet cord and gathering the family the condition of rescue.

options.text: A mark painted on the city gate.
options.note: The sign was a scarlet cord in her own window, not a mark on the city gate.

options.text: A password the spies gave her to shout during the battle.
options.note: There was no password; the sign was the visible scarlet cord in the window.

options.text: Nothing; the spies simply promised to remember her.
options.note: A concrete sign was required, the scarlet cord, along with keeping her family inside.

quiz.q: How does Rahab's rescue point forward in the Bible's story?

options.text: An outsider saved by faith, sheltered under a scarlet cord, foreshadowing the nations brought in and sinners sheltered by the blood of Christ.
options.note: Rahab enters Israel and the line of Jesus; the cord recalls the Passover blood, Matthew 1:5 and 1 Peter 1:19.

options.text: It shows that only ethnic Israelites could belong to God's people.
options.note: The opposite: a Canaanite is welcomed in, showing salvation reaches beyond Israel.

options.text: It teaches that God's judgment is never real, only a threat.
options.note: The judgment on Jericho was real; Rahab was genuinely rescued out from under it by faith.

options.text: It has no connection to the wider story of Scripture.
options.note: Rahab stands in the genealogy of Jesus and among the heroes of faith; her story is deeply connected.

quiz.q: What was the spies' report to Joshua?

options.text: The LORD had surely given the whole land into their hands, for its people were melting in fear.
options.note: Verse 24 records their faith-filled report, crediting the LORD for the coming victory.

options.text: The cities were too strong and the people too many to conquer.
options.note: That was the faithless report of the earlier spies in Numbers 13; these spies report confident faith.

options.text: Only Jericho could be taken, and no more.
options.note: They reported the entire land delivered into Israel's hands, not just Jericho.

options.text: Israel should turn back and settle east of the Jordan.
options.note: They urged advance, confident the LORD had given the land, not retreat.

questions: Rahab acted on the little she had heard about the LORD, while many in Israel with far more knowledge doubted. How are you responding to what God has already made known to you?
A Canaanite prostitute was welcomed into God's people and even into the line of Jesus. How does Rahab's story enlarge your sense of who God is willing to save, including yourself?
The scarlet cord marked the one safe place when judgment fell. Where are you tempted to find safety apart from the shelter God provides in Christ?
God's mighty acts had gone ahead of Israel, deciding the victory before the battle. Where do you need to trust that God has already gone before you into a situation you are facing?
Rahab risked everything to align herself with God's people against her own doomed city. Is there an old allegiance you need to leave behind to belong wholly to the LORD?

translationNotes.note: Shittim, where the spies are sent from, is also rendered Acacia Grove, as the BSB footnote notes; it was Israel's camp on the plains of Moab.
Devoted to destruction renders a form of the Hebrew cherem; the BSB footnote explains it as giving persons or things over to the LORD, by destroying or offering them.
The Red Sea is also rendered the Sea of Reeds, as the BSB footnote notes; it recalls the great deliverance of the exodus, Exodus 14.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Joshua 2:1-24. Prose narrative. No em or en dashes present in the source or introduced; none anywhere in the file. Sermon left as none; no sermon URL was verified this session.
